meta.workDescription:
Because the WSA is primarily an administrative environment but operates within a highly mobile and deployable Contingency Response Wing, this outline covers standard office hazards, emergency procedures, and exposure to mobility/flightline environments.

Supervisors must ensure this training is documented on the member's AF Form 55 (Employee Safety and Health Record) prior to the member performing any unsupervised tasks.
meta.references:
DAFI 91-202 - The Department of the Air Force (DAF) Mishap Prevention Program, The US Air Force Mishap Prevention Program
DFMAN 91-203, Air Force Occupational Safety, Fire and Health Standards
DAFI 90-802, Risk Management
AFI 90-821 - Hazard Communication (HAZCOM) Program
DAFMAN 91-203 - Air Force Occupational Safety, Fire and Health Standards
OSHA 2254 - Training Requirements in OSHA Standards
29 CFR 1910.1200 - Hazard Communication
29 CFR 1910.1030 - Bloodborne Pathogens
29 CFR 1910.151 - Medical services and first aid
AFRCI 41-104 - Professional Board and National Certification Examinations
29 CFR 1910.39 - Fire prevention plans
meta.riskManagementNotes:
1. Identify the Hazards: Spot the danger before it becomes a mishap (i.e., Noticing frayed electrical cords on a briefing room projector or ice on the main entrance steps).
2. Assess the Hazards: Determine the probability and severity of the risk (i.e., Evaluating that the icy steps have a High Probability of causing a Moderate Severity injury (slip/fall)).
3. Develop Controls & Make Decisions: Brainstorm ways to mitigate the risk and decide on a course of action	(i.e., Deciding to spread salt on the steps, reroute personnel to the back door, or put out warning cones).
4. Implement Controls: Put the chosen mitigation strategy into effect (i.e., Actually spreading the salt and putting up the "Wet Floor" or "Ice" warning signs).
5. Supervise & Evaluate: Ensure the controls are working and adjust if necessary (i.e., Checking the steps an hour later to see if the ice melted or if more salt is needed).
meta.bulletinBoard: Hallway near between restrooms on 1st floor.

moduleReferences: DAFMAN 91-203 - Air Force Occupational Safety, Fire and Health Standards
OSHA 2254 - Training Requirements in OSHA Standards
29 CFR 1910.151 - Medical services and first aid
AFRCI 41-104 - Professional Board and National Certification Examinations

selectedModules.title: Hazard Communication
selectedModules.reference: AFI 90-821 - Hazard Communication (HAZCOM) Program; 29 CFR 1910.1200 - Hazard Communication
selectedModules.trainingRequirement: Provide effective training at initial assignment and whenever a new chemical hazard is introduced. Cover where hazardous chemicals are present, the written program, labels and SDS access, how releases are detected, hazard types, protective measures, emergency procedures, and required PPE.
selectedModules.trainingSource: OSHA 2254 - Training Requirements in OSHA Standards / 29 CFR 1910.1200 - Hazard Communication
selectedModules.afTrainingRequirement: Employees potentially exposed to chemical hazards must complete HAZCOM training upon initial assignment and whenever a new hazard or chemical is introduced or new tasks create new hazards. Supervisors must ensure SDS access for chemicals used in the work process.
selectedModules.afTrainingSource: DAFMAN 91-203 - Air Force Occupational Safety, Fire and Health Standards para. 28.2.5 and para. 28.3.3

selectedModules.title: Bloodborne Pathogens
selectedModules.trainingRequirement: Train exposed employees at initial assignment and at least annually, with additional training when tasks or procedures create new exposure. Cover disease signs and transmission, the exposure control plan, task recognition, engineering controls, work practices, PPE use and disposal, and Hepatitis B vaccine information.
selectedModules.trainingSource: OSHA 2254 - Training Requirements in OSHA Standards / 29 CFR 1910.1030 - Bloodborne Pathogens
selectedModules.afTrainingRequirement: Any person whose duties involve reasonably anticipated exposure to blood or other potentially infectious material must be trained and enrolled in the Bloodborne Pathogen Program. Supervisors of affected workplaces must establish a written exposure control plan and conduct training.
selectedModules.afTrainingSource: DAFMAN 91-203 - Air Force Occupational Safety, Fire and Health Standards para. 1.9

selectedModules.title: CPR Training
selectedModules.reference: DAFMAN 91-203 - Air Force Occupational Safety, Fire and Health Standards Chapter 1
selectedModules.trainingRequirement: Where work conditions require trained first-aid responders, ensure personnel are adequately trained and available. OSHA 2254 links first-aid and CPR availability to certain work settings, but local mission risk and AF requirements should define who must hold current CPR credentials.
selectedModules.trainingSource: OSHA 2254 - Training Requirements in OSHA Standards / 29 CFR 1910.151 - Medical services and first aid and 1910.269; DAFMAN 91-203 - Air Force Occupational Safety, Fire and Health Standards
selectedModules.afTrainingRequirement: Initial first aid and CPR training must be completed before assigning duties that require it, refresher training must occur before certification expires, and CPR training must include PAD instruction. Remote or high-risk work must have an immediate medical response plan.
selectedModules.afTrainingSource: DAFMAN 91-203 - Air Force Occupational Safety, Fire and Health Standards para. 1.8

selectedModules.title: Fire Protection and Prevention
selectedModules.reference: Local extinguisher, pull station, and fire reporting procedures
selectedModules.trainingRequirement: Review the fire prevention plan with employees when the plan is developed, when responsibilities change, and when the plan changes. If extinguishers are provided for employee use, train employees on the general principles of extinguisher use and the hazards of incipient-stage firefighting at initial assignment and at least annually. Train designated users on the specific equipment they are expected to use.
selectedModules.trainingSource: OSHA 2254 - Training Requirements in OSHA Standards / 29 CFR 1910.39 - Fire prevention plans and 1910.157
selectedModules.afTrainingRequirement: Facility managers and supervisors must establish and maintain a fire prevention training program through the JSTO so employees understand their fire prevention and protection responsibilities in their work areas. This is fulfilled through job safety training and documentation under DAFI 91-202.
selectedModules.afTrainingSource: DAFMAN 91-203 - Air Force Occupational Safety, Fire and Health Standards para. 6.2.1.1

selectedModules.title: Ladder Safety
selectedModules.reference: Common local training item often broken out separately with fixed and portable ladder rules
selectedModules.trainingRequirement: Use local ladder lesson plans, manufacturer instructions, and AF walking-working surface guidance. OSHA 2254 (2015 edition) does not provide a dedicated general-industry ladder training summary in the same way it does for lockout, forklifts, or confined spaces, so this remains primarily AF/local-driven.
selectedModules.trainingSource: No direct OSHA 2254 - Training Requirements in OSHA Standards training excerpt; local procedures
selectedModules.afTrainingRequirement: Personnel using ladders at any working height must be trained when first assigned, and that training must include hands-on instruction covering ladder defects, electrocution hazards, positioning, and placement for various job sites. Training must be documented under DAFI 91-202.
selectedModules.afTrainingSource: DAFMAN 91-203 - Air Force Occupational Safety, Fire and Health Standards para. 7.4.3.7

selectedModules.title: Active Shooter Response
selectedModules.reference: Local emergency response briefing and lockdown procedures
selectedModules.trainingRequirement: Tie this to the local emergency action plan, shelter/lockdown locations, notification methods, and commander-directed response procedures.
